---
title: "yeg_opioid_report"
author: "Mike Zhang"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    nunmber-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    output-file: index.html
    output-dir: "/docs"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: fakse
---

```{r}
#| label: set-up

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)

edmonton_opioid <- readRDS("data/processed/edmonton_opioid.rds")
calgary_opioid<- readRDS("data/processed/calgary_opioid.rds")
edmonton_any <- readRDS("data/processed/edmonton_any.rds") 
calgary_any<- readRDS("data/processed/calgary_any.rds")

edmonton_opioid_final <- readRDS("models/edmonton_opioid_final.rds")
calgary_opioid_final <- readRDS("models/calgary_opioid_final.rds")
edmonton_any_final <- readRDS("models/edmonton_any_final.rds")
calgary_any_final <- readRDS("models/calgary_any_final.rds")

edmonton_opioid 

```

# Introduction

The implementation of supervised consumption sites (SCS) has been a contentious and heavily politicized public health topic in Alberta over the past few years. With the Alberta government having already closed some SCSs within the province and planning further closures in the near future, this project examines the association between SCS implementation and drug-related death rates in two major Alberta cities, Edmonton and Calgary, using segmented linear regression on time series data of opioid and all-substance death rates per 100,000 population. All data were scraped from the Alberta Substance Use Surveillance System and span January 2016 to March 2026. The main findings complicate a simple narrative because no statistically significant effect of SCS implementation was found in Calgary, for either opioid or all-substance death rates. In Edmonton, however, both opioid and all-substance death rates rose significantly faster following SCS implementation relative to control zones, and the near-identical effect across both outcomes suggests this reflects factors specific to Edmonton during this period rather than a mechanism attributable to SCS itself.

# Background

Supervised consumption sites (SCS) are health facilities where individuals can use pre-obtained drugs under the observation of trained staff, who can intervene with oxygen or naloxone if an overdose occurs. Naloxone specifically reverses opioid overdoses by blocking opioid receptors. It has no equivalent mechanism for cocaine, methamphetamine, alcohol, or most other substances. This mechanism matters for interpreting this project's findings, discussed further below.

Alberta's first SCS opened at Calgary's Sheldon M. Chumir Health Centre in October 2017, followed shortly after by sites in Edmonton (Boyle Street Community Services in March 2018, George Spady Centre in April 2018, and Boyle McCauley Health Centre in November 2018) and the Royal Alexandra Hospital's in-patient program. Over the following two years, sites also opened in several other Alberta cities: Lethbridge's ARCHES site opened February 28, 2018 (closed by the provincial government in 2020, later replaced by a separate site at the Lethbridge Shelter), Red Deer opened a site around September 2018 (closed March 2025), and Grande Prairie's mobile site opened March 11, 2019 and remains in operation. Lethbridge, Red Deer, and Grande Prairie fall within Alberta's South, Central, and North health zones respectively — the same three zones used as comparison groups in this analysis, a point revisited in the Methods section.

SCS implementation has remained politically contentious in Alberta since these initial openings, with debate centered around concerns about crime, social disorder, and needle debris in surrounding neighbourhoods, weighed against harm-reduction outcomes such as overdose reversals and reduced disease transmission. Under the current provincial government, policy has shifted decisively toward a "recovery-oriented" model emphasizing treatment and detox services over harm reduction. This shift has translated into concrete closures: the Royal Alexandra Hospital site in Edmonton closed in December 2025, and in March 2026 the government announced the closure of the Calgary Chumir site and the Lethbridge site, both effective June 30, 2026 — leaving only two sites in Edmonton and the Grande Prairie mobile site remaining in the province. The government has cited a report from the Canadian Centre of Recovery Excellence, a Crown corporation it funds, showing no increase in mortality following the Red Deer site's 2025 closure; critics have challenged both the report's independence and its conclusions, and some have pointed to rising drug poisoning deaths in Lethbridge following the 2020 closure of the ARCHES site as evidence of harm.

The broader evidence base on SCS effectiveness is itself mixed. International research, most notably on Vancouver's Insite facility, has generally reported reductions in overdose mortality and safer injection practices in surrounding areas. Evidence specific to Alberta and other Canadian jurisdictions is comparatively limited and contested, with government-commissioned reviews and independent research sometimes reaching different conclusions. Given the scale of the closures currently underway and the province's stated intention to move away from this model entirely, a locally-scoped, methodologically rigorous evaluation of SCS impact in Alberta's two largest cities is timely.

# Method

## Data

All the data analyzed were scraped from Alberta Substance Use Surveillance System (ASUSS)'s SAS VA dashboard under its "Acute substance deaths overview" tab using Google Chrome Developer Tools' Network tab to locate and download the underlying CSV export files. The monthly rates of drug poisoning deaths per 100,000 person years of all five AHS zones ranged from January 2016 to March 2026. Death rates of both any opioid and any substance were extracted as outcome variables for specificity check later. Among the zones, Edmonton and Calgary zones were modeled as treatment zones, while the other three (Central, North, South) were treated as control zones. For intervention starting dates, March 1st 2018 and October 1st 2017 were chosen for Edmonton and Calgary zones, respectively, because these were the months where each zone's first SCS opened, and no denominator information was provided in its accompanying documentation.

## Study Design

Controlled interrupted time series (ITS) was selected for this project as it fits our scenario where a known-date intervention with a comparison group is available. Since SCS intervention dates were different for Edmonton and Calgary zones, they were modeled separately instead of pooled, which would force one shared date across both treated zones and misrepresent Calgary's and Edmonton's actual staggered timing. Notably, other three zones that served as the control group also opened and closed their own SCS sites over the years, so the analysis is more like a staggered-rollout comparison rather than treated-vs-never-treated.

## Model Specification

The final segmented regression formula is `rate ~ time + zone + intervention + time_after + covid + covid_time` where `rate` represents drug-related death rates per 100,000 person-years, `time` represents months passed since the earliest date of the data, `zone` represents AHS zones, a binary `intervention` variable indicates whether SCS was in effect or not, `time_after` serves as months passed since the intervention dates (0 before the intervention, and for control zones throughout), another binary `covid` variable indicates whether COVID-19's lockdown, arbitrarily set to March 1st 2020, has happened, and `covid_time` represents months elapsed since COVID-19.

COVID-19 was modeled with both a level shift (`covid`) and a slope change (`covid_time`) rather than a level shift alone. This decision followed a specific diagnostic sequence where a formal linearity check (adding a quadratic term to `time_after` and testing it via likelihood ratio test) found significant curvature in the post-intervention segment for both Edmonton and Calgary. Visual inspection suggested this curvature coincided with the onset of COVID-19 rather than reflecting a genuine nonlinear SCS effect. To test this, `covid_time` was added to allow COVID-19 its own slope, separate from `time_after`. This resolved the issue since once `covid_time` was included, the quadratic term was no longer statistically significant, confirming that the apparent curvature was attributable to COVID-19's distinct trajectory rather than non-linearity in the SCS effect itself.

## Auto-correlation

To be able to run segmented linear regression models, autocorrelation among data points at different dates needed to be corrected. An Ordinary Least Squares model without correction would distort the standard errors of parameter coefficients, leading to unreliable significance tests, even though the coefficient estimates themselves would remain unbiased. Generalized Least Squares (GLS) models were utilized over other alternatives, such as Prais-Winsten and Newey-West/HAC, because the data had a panel structure with multiple zones where zone-specific correction was needed. GLS was also favored over Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE), a comparable panel-data alternative, because GLS's likelihood-based framework allowed the autocorrelation correction to be formally tested against a plain OLS model rather than simply assumed. The argument `method = "ML"` was included in `gls()` to fit AR(1)-corrected GLS models on a scale comparable to OLS models, and the two were compared using `anova()`, which returned statistically significant results for both treatment zones, justifying the AR(1) correction. All final models were run with `method = "REML"` for reporting.

## Assumption Checks

### Parallel Pre-trends

GLS models incorporating AR(1) correction using the formula `rate ~ time * zone` were run on each zone's pre-intervention period, bounded by that treated zone's own intervention date (`2017-10-01` for Calgary zone and `2018-03-01` for Edmonton zone). The results showed non-significant interaction terms for both Calgary and Edmonton against all three comparison zones, supporting the parallel pre-trends assumption.

### Linearity

Linearity was assessed both within the post-intervention segment specifically (via a quadratic term on time_after), and across the overall shared time trend (via a quadratic term on time), each tested against the baseline AR(1) model via likelihood ratio test. The post-intervention segment showed no significant curvature for either zone (Calgary: p = 0.256; Edmonton: p = 0.159), supporting a linear specification for the SCS effect itself. The overall trend, however, showed significant curvature for both zones (Calgary: p = 0.0003; Edmonton: p = 0.0002). This was investigated further and attributed to COVID-19's distinct trajectory rather than non-linearity in the SCS effect; see Model Specification for the resolution.

### Residual Diagnosis

QQ plots showed right-skewed residuals in the primary models, likely driven by small monthly death counts in low-population zones. Log-transforming the outcome variable was attempted to address this, but it introduced skewness in the opposite direction, traced to zone-months with a death rate of zero (e.g., South and North zones in several months), and additionally broke the parallel pre-trends assumption for the South zone specifically. Given these issues, the raw-scale model was retained as the primary specification, with the log-transformed model kept only as a secondary robustness check (see Sensitivity and Specificity Analyses). The residual skew in the primary model remains a disclosed limitation, likely reflecting the inherent instability of rate estimates built on small monthly death counts in lower-population zones.

## Sensitivity and Specificity Analyses

### Specificity Check

A specificity check was conducted by swapping the original outcome variable (any opioid death rates) for any-substance death rates. This tested mechanistic specificity to opioids, given SCS's naloxone-based mechanism, which has no plausible pathway to affect non-opioid deaths. Results were nearly identical to the baseline AR(1) models, particularly for Edmonton, suggesting a zone-wide confound rather than an SCS-specific effect.

### Sensitivity Analysis

Two sensitivity analyses were conducted. The first excluded the South zone from the comparison group, given that Lethbridge (South zone) opened its own SCS within weeks of Edmonton's, making South a compromised control. The second fit uncontrolled single-zone models with no comparison zones at all. Both single-zone models showed no statistically significant SCS effect. Under South-exclusion, Calgary's `time_after` coefficient flipped sign and became statistically significant; however, given the number of specifications tested across this analysis, this result should be interpreted with caution due to the risk of inflated Type I error from multiple comparisons. Both sensitivity analyses were conducted after the primary model was finalized, to avoid the appearance of post-hoc specification searching.

## Software

All analyses and visualizations were performed on R version 4.6.1. Packages utilized include `tidyverse` for general data manipulation and visualization (`ggplot2`), `lubridate` for date variables, and `nlme` for GLS modeling.
